section.title: To You I Lift Up My Soul
section.sectionRationale: An acrostic psalm of David, its lines following the order of the Hebrew alphabet as a sign of completeness and careful devotion. It moves between plea and confidence: David lifts his soul to the LORD, confesses the sins of his youth, and asks to be taught God's ways, grounding every request in God's covenant loving devotion and His goodness to sinners who fear Him.
section.thesis: David lifts up his soul in trust to the LORD, asking not for an easy path but for instruction in God's ways, pardon for his sin, and deliverance from his enemies, all resting on the changeless loving devotion of a God who guides sinners and confides in those who fear Him.
section.frameNote: Four movements: trust and a plea for guidance despite shame, enemies, and sin (1-7); the LORD's goodness in guiding sinners and the humble (8-11); covenant intimacy with those who fear Him (12-14); and a renewed plea for deliverance, closing with prayer for the whole nation (15-22).

units.label: To You, O LORD, I lift up my soul
units.note: trust and a plea for guidance despite shame, enemies, and sin

verses.text: To You, O LORD, I lift up my soul;
in You, my God, I trust. Do not let me be put to shame; do not let my enemies exult over me.
Surely none who wait for You will be put to shame; but those who are faithless without cause will be disgraced.
Show me Your {{derek|ways}}, O LORD; teach me Your paths.

Guide me in Your truth and teach me, for You are the God of my salvation; 
all day long I wait for You.
Remember, O LORD, Your compassion and {{chesed|loving devotion}}, for they are from age to age.
Remember not the sins of my youth, nor my rebellious acts; remember me according to Your loving devotion, because of Your goodness, O LORD.

units.label: Good and upright is the LORD
units.note: guidance for sinners and the humble

verses.text: Good and upright is the LORD; therefore He shows sinners the way.
He guides the humble in what is right and teaches them His way.
All the LORD's ways are loving and faithful to those who keep His covenant and His decrees.
For the sake of Your name, O LORD, forgive my iniquity, for it is great.

units.label: The LORD confides in those who fear Him

verses.text: Who is the man who fears the LORD? He will instruct him in the path chosen for him.
His soul will dwell in prosperity, and his descendants will inherit the land.
The LORD confides in those who fear Him, and reveals His {{berit|covenant}} to them.

units.label: Redeem Israel, O God
units.note: a renewed plea, widened to the whole nation

verses.text: My eyes are always on the LORD, for He will free my feet from the mesh.
Turn to me and be gracious, for I am lonely and afflicted.
The troubles of my heart increase; free me from my distress.
Consider my affliction and trouble, and take away all my sins.
Consider my enemies, for they are many, and they hate me with vicious hatred.

Guard my soul and deliver me; let me not be put to shame, 
for I take refuge in You.
May integrity and uprightness preserve me, because I wait for You.
Redeem Israel, O God, from all its distress.

derek.gloss: Way, road, path, often used of a manner of life or of God's own dealings and instruction. David's plea to be shown God's ways is a plea for moral and relational conformity to God, not merely information, fulfilled in Jesus, who calls Himself the way, John 14:6.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant loving devotion. David appeals to this quality of God, unchanging from age to age, as the ground for both forgiveness of his youthful sin and confidence that God's ways are trustworthy.

berit.gloss: Covenant, a binding relational commitment. The LORD reveals this covenant to those who fear Him, an intimacy of friendship fulfilled and deepened in the new covenant sealed in Christ's blood, Luke 22:20, Hebrews 8:10.

basic.context: This acrostic psalm follows the Hebrew alphabet line by line, a mark of careful, complete devotion rather than a hurried cry. David lifts his soul to the LORD, asking to be shown God's ways and taught His paths, confessing both present shame and the sins of his youth, and grounding every plea in God's changeless compassion and loving devotion. The psalm widens at its end from David's own trouble to a prayer for the redemption of the whole nation, Israel.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm should be read as a model prayer of humble dependence: guidance from God is not a reward for the sinless but a gift to sinners who fear Him, are humble, and keep covenant with Him. Its acrostic form signals that David means to bring his whole self, from alef to tav, before the LORD.
basic.originalLanguages: David asks to be shown God's derek, ways, and pleads on the basis of God's chesed, His steadfast covenant loving devotion, appealing to it both for pardon of his sins and for confidence in future guidance. The LORD's berit, covenant, is revealed as an intimate friendship to those who fear Him, not a mere legal arrangement.
basic.typology: David's plea to be taught God's ways anticipates Jesus, who does not merely point to the way but declares Himself to be the way, the truth, and the life, John 14:6. The covenant intimacy promised to those who fear the LORD, verse 14, is deepened and secured forever in the new covenant Christ establishes in His blood.
basic.christ: Jesus is the fulfillment of every plea in this psalm. He is the way David longed to be shown, John 14:6, the perfectly humble and upright one who was Himself taught by the Father and always did what pleased Him, John 8:29. He bears the guilt of iniquity great enough to require forgiveness, not His own but ours, and He seals a new and better covenant, revealed not only to the few who feared the LORD under David, but to all who trust in Him, Hebrews 8:10-12.
basic.god: God is shown as approachable to sinners, not despite His goodness but because of it: 'Good and upright is the LORD, therefore He shows sinners the way,' verse 8. His guidance is relational, not mechanical, offered to the humble and those who fear Him, and grounded in covenant loving devotion that reaches back to David's youth and forward to Israel's future redemption.
basic.crossReferences: John 14:6 shows Jesus as the way David sought to be shown. Hebrews 8:10-12 describes the new covenant in which God's ways are written on hearts and sins are remembered no more, the ultimate answer to David's plea in verse 7. Titus 2:11-12 shows God's grace itself teaching believers to live uprightly.
basic.oneStory: David's lifelong plea to be shown God's ways, forgiven his sin, and kept safe by covenant loving devotion finds its answer in Jesus Christ, who is Himself the way, who bears away the guilt too great to forgive on our own, and who seals forever the covenant intimacy David could only long for from a distance.

context.literary: An acrostic psalm of David, its twenty two lines corresponding to the Hebrew alphabet, blending elements of lament, wisdom instruction, and confident trust, unified by repeated appeals to God's ways, His covenant, and His loving devotion.
context.historical: Traditionally associated with David amid the pressures of enemies and personal guilt, reflecting the common experience of a king who is both under attack from without and aware of failure within, needing both deliverance and pardon.
context.canonical: Part of the wider wisdom and trust tradition of the Psalter, echoing Proverbs' theme that the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom and anticipating the New Covenant promise, developed fully in Jeremiah 31 and Hebrews 8, that God will reveal His ways intimately to His people.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To model humble, covenant-grounded prayer that asks God for instruction, pardon, and protection, resting not on personal merit but on God's own compassion, goodness, and covenant loving devotion.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's own prayer and confidence; it functions prescriptively as a pattern for every believer's approach to God, especially in seeking guidance and confessing long-standing sin.

debates.question: Is the guidance David seeks in verses 4-5 primarily a request for specific strategic direction in his immediate circumstances, or a broader request for moral and relational conformity to God's character?

views.label: David is seeking specific direction for a particular crisis, likely involving the enemies mentioned throughout the psalm, asking God to show him the practical path forward
views.case: The psalm repeatedly mentions enemies and distress, suggesting a concrete, situational need for guidance in the middle of real danger, not merely abstract moral instruction.

views.label: David's request is fundamentally for moral and spiritual conformity to God's character and ways, a lifelong orientation rather than a single decision
views.case: The verbs 'teach' and 'guide' paired with 'Your truth' and 'Your paths' point toward an ongoing, holistic transformation of David's whole way of life, consistent with the psalm's emphasis on humility and the fear of the LORD as prerequisites, not a single tactical answer.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm teaches that guidance flows from a right relationship with God rather than technique; whether the immediate need is tactical or lifelong, the prerequisite is the same humility, confession, and fear of the LORD that verses 8-14 describe.

study.originalLanguages: David's plea to be shown God's derek, ways, rests on appeal to God's chesed, His steadfast covenant loving devotion, unchanging from age to age; and the LORD's berit, covenant, is revealed as intimate friendship to those who fear Him, not a cold legal bond.

typology.type: Being shown and taught God's ways, verses 4-5, 8-9
typology.fulfillment: Jesus Christ, who declares Himself the way, the truth, and the life, John 14:6, and who was Himself perfectly taught and obedient to the Father, John 8:29.

typology.type: The LORD's covenant revealed to those who fear Him, verse 14
typology.fulfillment: The new covenant sealed in Christ's blood, in which God's ways are written on the heart and known intimately by all His people, Hebrews 8:10-12.

study.christ: Every plea of this psalm finds its answer in Jesus. He is the way David longed to be shown, John 14:6, and the one who perfectly walked in the LORD's paths. He bears away iniquity too great for David or any sinner to forgive on their own, and in His blood He establishes a covenant that reveals God's ways intimately to all who fear Him, not merely a chosen remnant, Hebrews 8:10-12. He is also the true and greater Israel, finally redeemed from all distress, the widened hope of the psalm's closing verse.
study.god: God is approachable to sinners because of His goodness, not despite it: 'Good and upright is the LORD, therefore He shows sinners the way,' verse 8. He guides the humble, forgives great iniquity for the sake of His own name, and reveals covenant intimacy to those who fear Him, a relational, personal guidance rather than a mechanical transaction.

crossReferences.note: Jesus declares Himself the way David sought to be shown, the ultimate answer to this psalm's central plea.
The new covenant promise that God's ways will be known intimately by His people and their sins remembered no more, fulfilling verse 7's plea and verse 14's promise.
God's grace itself teaches believers to renounce ungodliness and live uprightly, echoing this psalm's hope of divine instruction.

study.oneStory: David lifts his soul to the LORD, asking to be shown God's ways, forgiven his sin, and kept safe by covenant loving devotion. That prayer is answered fully in Jesus Christ, who is Himself the way, who bears away guilt too great to forgive on our own, and who seals forever, in a new covenant, the intimacy with God that David could only long for from a distance.

sermon.title: The Goodness of God and the Guidance of Sinners
sermon.why: Piper walks through Psalm 25's situation of fear, loneliness, guilt, and confusion, showing that the prerequisite for divine guidance is not a quest for messages but a quest for holiness, humility, covenant keeping, and faith, exactly the movement of this psalm.

quiz.q: What does David ask God not to remember, and what does he ask Him to remember, in verses 6-7?

options.text: Not to remember the sins of his youth and rebellious acts, but to remember him according to God's loving devotion and goodness.
options.note: David explicitly contrasts these in verse 7, appealing to God's character rather than his own record.

options.text: Not to remember his sins in general, but to remember his prayers.
options.note: Close, since forgetting sin is half the picture, but verse 7 specifically grounds remembrance in God's loving devotion and goodness, not in the merit of David's prayers.

options.text: Not to remember his youth at all, but to remember his old age.
options.note: This misreads 'sins of my youth' as merely a life stage rather than specific transgressions requiring forgiveness.

options.text: Not to remember Israel's history, but to remember David's kingship.
options.note: The psalm's concern here is personal sin and God's character, not national history or David's throne.

quiz.q: According to verses 8-9, whom does the LORD guide and instruct in His way?

options.text: Sinners and the humble.
options.note: Verse 8 says He shows sinners the way, and verse 9 says He guides the humble; sinlessness is never presented as a qualification for guidance.

options.text: Only kings and rulers.
options.note: The text specifies sinners and the humble, not a particular social class or office.

options.text: Those who show at least some humility, even if still sinful.
options.note: Close, since humility is named in verse 9, but this option narrows the scope; the text also explicitly names sinners generally in verse 8, not only the partly humble.

options.text: Israel's priests exclusively.
options.note: The psalm speaks broadly of sinners and the humble, not a priestly office specifically.

quiz.q: What does verse 14 reveal about the LORD's disposition toward those who fear Him?

options.text: He confides in them, an intimate friendship, and reveals His covenant to them.
options.note: This verse pictures a relational closeness, not merely rule-following, between God and those who fear Him.

options.text: He keeps them at a distance until they prove themselves.
options.note: The verse describes confiding intimacy, the opposite of distance.

options.text: He reveals His covenant only to prophets.
options.note: The verse specifies those who fear Him generally, not a prophetic office specifically.

options.text: He tests them without ever drawing near.
options.note: While the fear of the LORD does involve reverence, this option misses the explicit intimacy of 'confides' and 'reveals.'

quiz.q: What is the psalm's final petition in verse 22?

options.text: That God would redeem Israel from all its distress, widening David's personal prayer to the whole covenant community.
options.note: The psalm ends by broadening from David's individual plea to the nation as a whole.

options.text: That God would give David greater wealth.
options.note: The final verse is about national redemption from distress, not personal wealth.

options.text: That God would deliver David personally, without mentioning the nation.
options.note: Close, since personal deliverance runs through the whole psalm, but verse 22 specifically widens the request to all Israel, not David alone.

options.text: That David alone would be spared, apart from the nation.
options.note: The verse explicitly widens the scope to all Israel, the opposite of sparing David apart from the nation.

quiz.q: What does this psalm teach about the relationship between guidance and holiness?

options.text: Guidance is not earned by sinlessness but is given to those who humble themselves, fear the LORD, and keep covenant with Him.
options.note: Verses 8-14 make clear that humility and the fear of the LORD, not a flawless record, are the conditions for God's guidance.

options.text: Guidance is only available to those who have never sinned.
options.note: The psalm explicitly says the LORD shows sinners the way, verse 8.

options.text: Guidance requires strict religious performance rather than a humble, fearing heart.
options.note: Close, since covenant keeping is named in verse 10, but the psalm frames this as sincere fear and humility rather than flawless religious performance.

options.text: Guidance is unrelated to a person's spiritual condition.
options.note: The psalm ties guidance directly to humility and the fear of the LORD, so spiritual condition is central, not irrelevant.

questions: David asks God to remember His loving devotion rather than the sins of his youth. What sins from your own past do you find hardest to believe God has truly forgiven?
Verse 8 says God shows sinners the way because He is good and upright, not despite it. How does this change the way you approach God when you need guidance but feel disqualified by your own failures?

The psalm ties guidance to humility and the fear of the LORD rather than technique or special messages. Where in your life are you seeking a shortcut to guidance instead of the slower path of humility and holiness?

Verse 14 pictures the LORD confiding in those who fear Him, an intimate friendship. How well does your own picture of God match this kind of relational nearness, rather than a distant, transactional one?

The psalm widens from David's personal plea to a prayer for the whole nation's redemption. Who in your circle of family, friends, or community needs you to carry them in prayer the way David carried Israel?

translationNotes.note: Loving devotion renders chesed, God's steadfast covenant love, appealed to here as unchanging from age to age, the ground for both pardon and confidence.
Sins of my youth likely refers to specific earlier failures rather than a general reference to youthfulness, since David asks God to remember him instead 'according to Your loving devotion.'

Confides renders a Hebrew word for intimate counsel or friendship, the same idea used of close, trusted relationships, picturing God's covenant revealed as personal nearness rather than distant information.
